questions.text: Proliferative phase occurs in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Following menstruation, the proliferative phase begins. It usually lasts from around day 6 to day 14 of a 28-day cycle, but the exact timing can vary. During this phase: Follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) stimulates the development of ovarian follicles, each containing an immature egg (oocyte). Rising levels of estrogen, produced by the developing follicles, stimulate the growth and proliferation of the endometrial lining in the uterus.

Menstruation occurs during the first few days of the menstrual cycle. The uterine lining, or endometrium, sheds if no pregnancy occurred during the previous cycle. Bleeding typically lasts for 3-7 days
Ovulation is the release of a mature egg from the ovary into the fallopian tube, making it available for fertilizatization. The ovulatory phase typically occurs around day 14 in a 28-day cycle.

After ovulation (which usually occurs around day 14 in a 28-day cycle), the ruptured follicle transforms into a structure called the corpus luteum. The corpus luteum produces progesterone, which helps prepare the endometrial lining for potential implantation of a fertilized egg. So, on days 20-28, you would be in the latter part of the luteal phase, approaching the end of the menstrual cycle.

questions.explanation: Following menstruation, the proliferative phase begins. It usually lasts from around day 6 to day 14 of a 28-day cycle, but the exact timing can vary. During this phase: Follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) stimulates the development of ovarian follicles, each containing an immature egg (oocyte). Rising levels of estrogen, produced by the developing follicles, stimulate the growth and proliferation of the endometrial lining in the uterus.

questions.text: The follicular phase of the menstruation cycle occurs:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Luteal Phase (Days 15-28): After ovulation, the ruptured follicle transforms into a structure called the corpus luteum. The corpus luteum produces progesterone, which prepares the endometrial lining for possible implantation of a fertilized egg. If fertilization doesn't occur, the corpus luteum degenerates, leading to a decrease in progesterone and the initiation of the next menstrual phase.
Menstruation occurs during the first few days of the menstrual cycle.

Ovulation (Around Day 14): Midway through the menstrual cycle, a surge in luteinizing hormone (LH) occurs. This surge triggers the release of the mature egg (ovum) from the dominant follicle into the fallopian tube, marking the end of the follicular phase and the beginning of the ovulatory phase.

Follicular Phase (Days 6-14): The early follicular phase overlaps with the menstrual phase. After menstruation, the ovaries begin to develop several ovarian follicles, each containing an immature egg (oocyte). Follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) stimulates the growth of these follicles. As the follicles grow, they produce increasing amounts of estrogen. As estrogen levels rise, it stimulates the thickening and growth of the endometrial lining in the uterus. One dominant follicle usually becomes the primary focus, and the others degenerate. The rising estrogen levels also contribute to the negative feedback loop on FSH, preventing the development of additional follicles.

questions.explanation: Follicular Phase (Days 6-14): The early follicular phase overlaps with the menstrual phase. After menstruation, the ovaries begin to develop several ovarian follicles, each containing an immature egg (oocyte). Follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) stimulates the growth of these follicles. As the follicles grow, they produce increasing amounts of estrogen. As estrogen levels rise, it stimulates the thickening and growth of the endometrial lining in the uterus. One dominant follicle usually becomes the primary focus, and the others degenerate. The rising estrogen levels also contribute to the negative feedback loop on FSH, preventing the development of additional follicles.

questions.text: Sperms are produced by testes and stored in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The seminal vesicles are a pair of small glands located behind the bladder and near the base of the prostate gland in males. These glands play a crucial role in the production of semen. The seminal vesicles secrete a significant portion of the fluid that makes up semen, which nourishes and helps transport sperm during ejaculation.
The scrotum is the pouch of skin and muscle that houses and protects the testes (testicles) in males. It is located below the penis.

The prostate is a walnut-sized gland that is part of the male reproductive system. It is located just below the bladder and surrounds the urethra, the tube that carries urine and semen out of the body. The prostate gland produces a fluid that, along with sperm from the testicles and fluids from other glands, makes up semen.

Sperm is produced in the testes, specifically in structures called the seminiferous tubules. After production, the sperm undergoes maturation and is then transported to the epididymis for storage and further development.

questions.explanation: Sperm is produced in the testes, specifically in structures called the seminiferous tubules. After production, the sperm undergoes maturation and is then transported to the epididymis for storage and further development.

questions.text: The term 'viviparous animals' means:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Internal fertilization leads to internal development of the embryo
options.explanation: Animals that give birth to offspring are called viviparous. In viviparous animals, both fertilization, as well as the development of the embryo, takes place inside the female reproductive system. Eg-Dolphin, Whales, Humans, Lion, Tiger, Elephant, Dogs, etc give birth to young ones.

Animals that give birth to offspring are called viviparous. In viviparous animals, both fertilization, as well as the development of the embryo, takes place inside the female reproductive system. Eg-Dolphin, Whales, Humans, Lion, Tiger, Elephant, Dogs, etc give birth to young ones.

Animals that give birth to offspring are called viviparous. In viviparous animals, both fertilization, as well as the development of the embryo, takes place inside the female reproductive system. Eg-Dolphin, Whales, Humans, Lion, Tiger, Elephant, Dogs, etc give birth to young ones.

options.text: Organisms that store infants in pouches

questions.explanation: Animals that have embryos develop inside their body are called ''viviparous animals''. On the other hand, animals that lay eggs are called ''oviparous animals''.

questions.text: From day 6-28 of a menstrual cycle, which wall of the uterus is repaired?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: From day 6-28 of the menstrual cycle, the repair and thickening of the endometrial lining occur.
The outermost layer of the uterus is called the perimetrium. It is a serous membrane that covers the outer surface of the uterus.
The middle layer of the uterus is the myometrium. It is composed of smooth muscle tissue. The myometrium is responsible for the powerful contractions of the uterus during labor and delivery.

The innermost layer of the uterus is called the endometrium. This lining undergoes cyclic changes during the menstrual cycle in response to hormonal fluctuations. During the menstrual phase, the superficial layer of the endometrium is shed if no pregnancy occurs. In the proliferative phase, under the influence of estrogen, the endometrium thickens and becomes more vascularized in preparation for a potential pregnancy. In the secretory phase, after ovulation, progesterone further stimulates the development of the endometrial glands and blood vessels, creating a more receptive environment for a fertilized egg.

questions.explanation: From day 6-28 of the menstrual cycle, the repair and thickening of the endometrial lining occur. This phase is part of the menstrual cycle and involves the proliferative and secretory phases. After menstruation (shedding of the previous cycle's endometrial lining), the proliferative phase begins. Rising levels of estrogen, produced by developing ovarian follicles, stimulate the growth and proliferation of the endometrial lining. Midway through the menstrual cycle, ovulation occurs, marked by the release of a mature egg from the ovary. After ovulation, the ruptured follicle transforms into a structure called the corpus luteum. The corpus luteum produces progesterone, which further supports the development and vascularization of the endometrial lining. The secretory phase prepares the endometrium for the possible implantation of a fertilized egg. If fertilization does not occur, the corpus luteum degenerates, leading to a decline in progesterone levels. This hormonal change triggers the shedding of the endometrial lining, and menstruation begins again, marking the start of a new menstrual cycle.

questions.text: The hormone responsible for placenta formation is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Serotonin is a neurotransmitter that primarily functions in the central nervous system. It also has roles outside the nervous system, such as in the gastrointestinal tract.

Estrogen is a group of sex hormones, with estradiol being the most potent form. Estrogen plays a crucial role in the development and maintenance of female reproductive tissues, but it is also present in males in smaller amounts.

After fertilization and the formation of a zygote, the developing embryo starts producing hCG. This hormone is essential for maintaining the corpus luteum, a structure formed from the ruptured follicle after ovulation. The corpus luteum, in turn, continues to produce progesterone, which helps sustain the early pregnancy. As the embryo implants into the uterus, the trophoblast cells, a layer of cells surrounding the developing embryo, play a crucial role in forming the placenta. These trophoblast cells produce hCG, and it is the rising levels of hCG that prevent the corpus luteum from degenerating. The sustained production of progesterone from the corpus luteum, in collaboration with other hormones, supports the early development of the placenta.
Insulin is a hormone produced by the pancreas that plays a central role in glucose metabolism and blood sugar regulation.

questions.explanation: After fertilization and the formation of a zygote, the developing embryo starts producing hCG. This hormone is essential for maintaining the corpus luteum, a structure formed from the ruptured follicle after ovulation. The corpus luteum, in turn, continues to produce progesterone, which helps sustain the early pregnancy. As the embryo implants into the uterus, the trophoblast cells, a layer of cells surrounding the developing embryo, play a crucial role in forming the placenta. These trophoblast cells produce hCG, and it is the rising levels of hCG that prevent the corpus luteum from degenerating. The sustained production of progesterone from the corpus luteum, in collaboration with other hormones, supports the early development of the placenta.

questions.text: Fusion of male and female gametes is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Fertilization is the process where the male and female gametes (sperm and egg) fuse to form a zygote. This marks the beginning of the development of a new organism.
Implantation is the process by which the developing embryo embeds itself into the lining of the uterus, securing a connection for nourishment and growth.
Development refers to the process of growth and differentiation of cells and tissues to form an organism. It encompasses various stages from fertilization to maturity.
Growth is the process of increasing in size or mass. While an essential aspect of development, it does not specifically refer to the fusion of gametes.

questions.text: Days 15-28 of the menstrual cycle are termed as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Bleeding phase is also known as menustration. This is the first phase of the menstrual cycle and involves the shedding of the endometrial lining if no pregnancy occurred in the previous cycle.

The secretory phase is part of the menstrual cycle and occurs after ovulation. This phase is characterized by the activity of the corpus luteum, which forms from the ruptured follicle after releasing an egg during ovulation. The secretory phase typically spans approximately days 15-28 of a 28-day menstrual cycle.

The pre-ovulatory phase, also known as the follicular phase, is the period of the menstrual cycle that occurs before ovulation. This phase encompasses the menstrual phase and the period leading up to the release of an egg from the ovary.

The proliferative phase is a stage of the menstrual cycle that occurs in the uterus, specifically in the endometrial lining. It follows the menstrual phase and precedes ovulation. The proliferative phase is characterized by the growth and thickening of the endometrial lining in preparation for a potential pregnancy.

questions.explanation: The secretory phase is part of the menstrual cycle and occurs after ovulation. This phase is characterized by the activity of the corpus luteum, which forms from the ruptured follicle after releasing an egg during ovulation. The secretory phase typically spans approximately days 15-28 of a 28-day menstrual cycle.

questions.text: Urine and sperms are able to pass through:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The uterus is a reproductive organ in females. It is a hollow, muscular organ located in the pelvic cavity between the bladder and the rectum.

In males, the urethra serves a dual purpose, allowing both urine and semen (containing sperm) to pass through. The segment of the urethra that carries urine is distinct from the segment that carries semen. This shared pathway is known as the male urethra.

The epididymis is a coiled tube that is part of the male reproductive system. It is located on the surface of each testis (testicle) and serves as a site for the maturation, storage, and transport of sperm. The epididymis is a crucial structure in the process of sperm development.
The ureters are tubular structures that connect the kidneys to the bladder. Each kidney has a ureter, and their primary function is to transport urine from the kidneys to the bladder.

questions.explanation: In males, the urethra serves a dual purpose, allowing both urine and semen (containing sperm) to pass through. The segment of the urethra that carries urine is distinct from the segment that carries semen. This shared pathway is known as the male urethra.

questions.text: To form a female zygote, the sperm cell must contribute which chromosome?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In humans, if the sex chromosomes are XX then the zygote is female and if the sex chromosomes are XY then it results in a male zygote. Females can therefore also only form ovum with an X chromosome. To form a female zygote, the sperm must donate another X chromosome to the ovum to form XX zygote again.

This option is incorrect. To form a female zygote, the sperm cell must contribute a sex chromosome carrying an X chromosome. In humans, the sex chromosomes are labeled as X and Y. Females have two X chromosomes (XX), and males have one X and one Y chromosome (XY).

questions.text: After fertilization the zygote increases in size and travels down the fallopian tube to become embedded in the walls of the womb. This process is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: release of egg from ovum is called ovulation

After fertilization, the zygote undergoes a series of cell divisions, forming a structure known as a blastocyst. The blastocyst then travels down the fallopian tube and eventually reaches the uterus. The process by which the blastocyst becomes embedded in the walls of the uterus is called implantation. Implantation is a critical step in the early stages of pregnancy. The blastocyst adheres to the endometrial lining of the uterus and starts to invade the tissue. This process establishes a connection between the developing embryo and the maternal blood supply, allowing for the exchange of nutrients and waste products.
Conception happens when sperm swims up through the vagina and fertilizes an egg in the fallopian tube
Menstruation, or period, is normal vaginal bleeding that occurs as part of a woman's monthly cycle.

questions.explanation: After fertilization, the zygote undergoes a series of cell divisions, forming a structure known as a blastocyst. The blastocyst then travels down the fallopian tube and eventually reaches the uterus. The process by which the blastocyst becomes embedded in the walls of the uterus is called implantation. Implantation generally occurs around 6-10 days after fertilization. The embryo, at this stage, is a multicellular structure, and the process of implantation marks the beginning of the embryonic stage of development. Once implanted, the cells of the blastocyst continue to differentiate, and the early structures of the developing fetus begin to form.

questions.text: The number of spermatid produced from primary spermatocyte is?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The primary spermatocyte undergoes meiosis. So, the number of spermatids produced cannot be 1. Thus, this is not the correct option.
The primary spermatocyte undergoes meiosis. So, the number of spermatids produced cannot be 2. Thus, this is not the correct option.
The primary spermatocyte undergoes meiosis. So, the number of spermatids produced cannot be 3. Thus, this is not the correct option.

During spermatogenesis, which is the process of sperm cell development, a primary spermatocyte undergoes two rounds of cell division to produce four haploid cells called spermatids. This process ensures that the resulting sperm cells (spermatozoa) have half the number of chromosomes as the original cell. The spermatids then undergo further maturation to become mature sperm cells.

questions.text: What is an example of an oviparous mammal?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Penguins lay eggs this means they are oviparous animals.
Bats are viviparous animals as they have internal fertilization and gestation after which they give birth to young, living pups.

Whales are viviparous animals and reproduce sexually. They have internal fertilization and gestation from 9 to 17 months. They give birth to their calf in water with the tail coming out first and she brings them to the surface of the water for their first breath.
Elephants reproduce sexually. They have a gestation period of 22 months and give birth on land.

questions.explanation: Penguins are oviparous, which means they lay eggs. Penguins lay eggs and incubating them. After mating, the female penguin lays one or two eggs, depending on the species, and then both the male and female take turns incubating the eggs by sitting on them to keep them warm. Once the eggs hatch, the parents continue to care for the chicks until they are ready to survive on their own. Whales, Bats and Elephants all reproduce sexually.

questions.text: Fluid is secreted by three sets of glands which combine with the sperm to form:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Fluid found in the spaces around cells. It comes from substances that leak out of blood capillaries (the smallest type of blood vessel). It helps bring oxygen and nutrients to cells and to remove waste products from them.

The fluid secreted by three sets of glands that combines with sperm to form semen includes contributions from the seminal vesicles, prostate gland, and bulbourethral (Cowper's) glands. These three sets of glands are part of the male reproductive system, and their secretions collectively contribute to the composition of semen.
Amniotic fluid is a clear, slightly yellowish liquid that surrounds the unborn baby (fetus) during pregnancy. It is contained in the amniotic sac.
This option is incorrect as the correct option is B.

These three sets of glands are part of the male reproductive system, and their secretions collectively contribute to the composition of semen.

questions.text: In mammals that are seasonal breeders, females are receptive only once a year. This is known as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The follicular phase, also known as the pre-ovulatory phase or proliferative phase, is the phase of the estrous cycle during which follicles in the ovary mature from primary follicle to a fully mature graafian follicle. It ends with ovulation.

In mammals that are seasonal breeders, females are receptive to mating only once a year during a specific period. This behavior is known as "seasonal estrus" or "seasonal heat." During the receptive period, females exhibit behaviors and physiological changes indicating their readiness to mate. The seasonal estrus, also spelled as the oestrous cycle, is the reproductive cycle that occurs in most mammalian females, excluding humans and certain primates. It consists of a series of hormonal and physiological changes that prepare the female for reproduction.
The luteal phase of menstrual cycle occurs right after ovulation (when ovary releases an egg). It lasts about 14 days and ends when menstrual period starts.

The menstrual cycle is a natural process. It is a complex cycle controlled by female hormones that cause regular bleeding (periods). The menstrual cycle has four phases: menstruation, the follicular phase, ovulation and the luteal phase. Some women may experience menstrual problems (e.g. heavy bleeding).

questions.text: All of the following statements are correct except:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The testicles produce millions of sperm
options.explanation: This statement is correct. The testes (testicles) are responsible for spermatogenesis, the process of sperm production.

options.text: Hormones are produced by the testicles
options.explanation: This statement is correct. The testicles produce hormones, including testosterone, which plays a crucial role in male reproductive development and function.

options.text: Semen is produced in the seminal vesicles
options.explanation: Semen is not produced in the seminal vesicles alone. Semen is a mixture of sperm and various fluids, and while seminal vesicles contribute fluids to semen, it is also produced by other accessory glands such as the prostate gland and bulbourethral glands.

options.text: All males are born with one testicle
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. Males are typically born with two testicles. The condition of having only one testicle is known as monorchism, and it is relatively uncommon.

questions.text: Which term refers to the formation of egg cells that begins in the developing ovaries of a female fetus?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Meiosis is a special type of cell division of germ cells in sexually-reproducing organisms that produces the gametes, such as sperm or egg cells. It involves two rounds of division that ultimately result in four cells with only one copy of each chromosome.
Fertilization is defined as the union of two gametes. During fertilization, sperm and egg fuse to form a diploid zygote to initiate prenatal development.
Release of egg from ovum is called ovulation.

The term that refers to the formation of egg cells (ova) that begins in the developing ovaries of a female fetus is "oogenesis." Oogenesis is the process by which ova (egg cells) are produced. It starts during fetal development and continues throughout the reproductive years of a female's life.

questions.explanation: The term that refers to the formation of egg cells (ova) that begins in the developing ovaries of a female fetus is "oogenesis." Oogenesis is the process by which ova (egg cells) are produced. It starts during fetal development and continues throughout the reproductive years of a female's life.

questions.text: Which characteristic is not of identical twins?

options.text: Produced by separation of two blastomeres

All offspring asexually reproduce are always identical therefore they will be twins and hence this answer is wrong.

All twins always have the same genetic material in their DNA, that's why is option is correct.

questions.text: During the menstrual cycle, which of the following events happens if a released egg does not become fertilized?

options.text: The lining of the womb wall stays built up
options.explanation: This option is not correct because the lining of the womb wall, also known as the endometrium, breaks down during menstruation.

questions.text: The female gametes are most commonly referred to as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The female gametes are most commonly referred to as eggs or ova (singular: ovum). These are the reproductive cells produced by the ovaries in females. When fertilized by a male sperm cell, an egg develops into a zygote, initiating the process of embryonic development.

questions.text: What is the name of the tube that carries sperm and urine out of the human body?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The penis is the male external genital organ, but it does not serve as a duct for carrying urine or sperm. It houses the urethra and facilitates the release of semen during ejaculation.

The urethra is a duct that serves as a common passage for both urine from the bladder and semen (containing sperm) from the reproductive system. It extends through the penis and allows for the elimination of both substances.
The seminal vesicle is a gland that contributes fluids to semen but does not function as a tube for carrying sperm or urine.
The ureter is a duct that carries urine from the kidneys to the bladder and is not involved in carrying sperm or facilitating the elimination of semen.

questions.text: This carries the egg to the uterus.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The structure that carries the egg from the ovary to the uterus is the fallopian tube, also known as the oviduct. After ovulation, the released egg travels through the fallopian tube, where it may encounter sperm for fertilization. If fertilization occurs, the resulting zygote will continue to develop as it moves down the fallopian tube toward the uterus, where it eventually implants into the uterine lining. If fertilization does not occur, the egg will not be fertilized and will be expelled from the body during menstruation.

Ovary is the small organ located on either side of the uterus. They produce and release eggs during the menstrual cycle. Ovaries also produce hormones (estrogen and progesterone) that regulate the menstrual cycle and fertility.

Cervix is lower, narrow part of the uterus that connects to the vagina. It forms a canal for menstrual blood to exit the uterus and allows sperm to enter during sexual intercourse. During pregnancy, the cervix remains closed and later dilates during labor.

Vagina is muscular canal connecting the uterus to the external opening. It serves as the birth canal and allows for the entry of the penis during sexual intercourse. The vagina is lined with a mucus membrane and can stretch to accommodate various processes such as intercourse, childbirth, and menstruation.

questions.explanation: The structure that carries the egg from the ovary to the uterus is the fallopian tube, also known as the oviduct. After ovulation, the released egg travels through the fallopian tube, where it may encounter sperm for fertilization. If fertilization occurs, the resulting zygote will continue to develop as it moves down the fallopian tube toward the uterus, where it eventually implants into the uterine lining. If fertilization does not occur, the egg will not be fertilized and will be expelled from the body during menstruation.

questions.text: Between the seminiferous tubules are interstitial cells which secrete which of the following hormone?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Progesterone is secreted by ovarian corpus lutetum.
Estrogen is secreted by ovaries.

Between the seminiferous tubules in the testes are interstitial cells, also known as Leydig cells. Leydig cells are responsible for secreting the male sex hormone testosterone. Testosterone plays a crucial role in the development and maintenance of male reproductive tissues and the expression of male secondary sexual characteristics. It influences the development of the testes, promotes the production of sperm in the seminiferous tubules, and is involved in the development of male secondary sexual characteristics such as facial hair, deepening of the voice, and increased muscle mass.

During labor and childbirth, oxytocin is released to stimulate uterine contractions, aiding in the delivery of the baby. Oxytocin also plays a role in the letdown reflex during breastfeeding. It causes the contraction of smooth muscles around the mammary glands, facilitating the release of milk.

questions.explanation: Between the seminiferous tubules in the testes are interstitial cells, also known as Leydig cells. Leydig cells are responsible for secreting the male sex hormone testosterone. Testosterone plays a crucial role in the development and maintenance of male reproductive tissues and the expression of male secondary sexual characteristics. It influences the development of the testes, promotes the production of sperm in the seminiferous tubules, and is involved in the development of male secondary sexual characteristics such as facial hair, deepening of the voice, and increased muscle mass.

questions.text: Sperms are nourished and activated through?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The vas deferens transports mature sperm cells from the epididymis to the urethra, where they are expelled from the body during ejaculation.

questions.text: When puberty takes place in girls, which one of the following does not occur?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: During puberty, both boys and girls undergo physical changes due to hormonal changes that take place in their bodies. One of the changes that occur during puberty is that the body starts to develop more muscle mass, and the bones grow in length and width. In general, during puberty, girls do experience some broadening of their shoulders due to the increase in muscle mass and bone growth. However, this broadening is usually not as significant as in boys because girls' bodies have lower levels of testosterone, which is a hormone that contributes to muscle development and bone growth.
One of the early signs of puberty is the development of breast buds, followed by the growth of breast tissue.

The onset of puberty is triggered by hormonal changes, specifically the increased production of estrogen. These hormonal shifts lead to the development of secondary sexual characteristics and the maturation of the reproductive system.

questions.text: What is the name of the tube that carries the sperm from the testes to the urethra?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The penis is the male reproductive organ involved in the passage of urine. It consists of three main parts: the root, which attaches to the pelvic bone; the shaft, which is the long and cylindrical part; and the glans, which is the rounded tip at the end of the shaft. The opening at the tip of the glans is called the urethral meatus, through which urine and semen pass.

The seminal vesicles are a pair of small glands located near the male reproductive organs, specifically the prostate gland and the base of the bladder. They play a crucial role in the production of semen. Seminal vesicles secrete a fluid that contains various substances, including fructose (a sugar that provides energy for sperm), prostaglandins (which aid in the contraction of the female reproductive tract), and other components that nourish and protect the sperm. During ejaculation, the fluid from the seminal vesicles mixes with sperm from the testes and secretions from the prostate gland to form semen. This semen is then ejaculated from the penis during sexual intercourse. The seminal vesicles are essential for male fertility as they provide a medium for the sperm to travel and survive in the female reproductive tract.

The prostate gland is a small, walnut-sized gland located just below the bladder in males. It is an integral part of the male reproductive system and plays a crucial role in semen production. The primary function of the prostate gland is to produce a fluid that makes up a significant portion of semen. This fluid contains enzymes, citric acid, and prostate-specific antigen (PSA), among other substances. The PSA helps to liquefy the semen after ejaculation, aiding the movement of sperm. During sexual arousal, the prostate gland releases its fluid into the urethra, which then combines with sperm from the testes and fluid from the seminal vesicles to form semen. This semen is ejaculated through the penis during sexual intercourse.

The sperm duct, also known as the vas deferens, is a muscular tube that carries sperm from the epididymis (where sperm are stored after they are produced in the testes) to the urethra. During ejaculation, the sperm are propelled through the sperm duct and mixed with seminal fluid from the seminal vesicles, prostate gland, and bulbourethral gland, to form semen, which is then released through the penis.

questions.explanation: The tube that carries sperm from the testes to the urethra is called the vas deferens. The vas deferens is a long, muscular tube that functions as part of the male reproductive system. During ejaculation, sperm move from the testes through the vas deferens, where they mix with fluids from the seminal vesicles, prostate gland, and bulbourethral gland to form semen. The semen then travels through the urethra and is expelled from the body through the penis.

questions.text: In human females, the release of the ovum from the follicle is called:
questions.subject: Biology

Fertilization is when the sperm and egg fuses. Therefore, is not the correct option.

Implantation is when a fertilized cell attaches to the uterine lining.

questions.explanation: The release of the ovum (egg) from the follicle in human females is called ovulation. Ovulation is a key event in the menstrual cycle and typically occurs around the middle of the menstrual cycle, roughly 14 days before the start of the next menstrual period. During ovulation, a mature follicle in the ovary ruptures, releasing the egg into the fallopian tube, where it can potentially be fertilized by sperm. If fertilization does not occur, the egg will disintegrate, and the menstrual cycle will proceed with the shedding of the uterine lining during menstruation.

questions.text: During birth, which of following act as birth canal?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The tube through which an ovum or egg passes from an ovary is called oviduct.

Ovary is one of a pair of female glands in which the eggs form and the female hormones estrogen and progesterone are made. These hormones play an important role in female traits, such as breast development, body shape, and body hair. They are also involved in the menstrual cycle, fertility, and pregnancy.
The hollow, pear-shaped organ in a woman's pelvis is uterus. The uterus is where a fetus (unborn baby) develops and grows.

During birth, the birth canal refers to the pathway through which the baby passes from the uterus to the outside world. In humans, the birth canal is composed of two main structures: Cervix: The cervix is the lower, narrow part of the uterus that connects to the vagina. During labor, the cervix dilates (opens) to allow the baby to pass through. Vagina: The vagina is the muscular tube that extends from the cervix to the external genitalia. It serves as the passageway for the baby during delivery. As labor progresses, contractions of the uterus and the muscles surrounding the birth canal help push the baby through the cervix and into the vagina, ultimately leading to childbirth. The process of labor and delivery is a complex physiological event that involves the coordinated efforts of both the mother's body and the baby's positioning for a successful passage through the birth canal.

questions.text: Which method from the following is an example of asexual reproduction?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Spores are cells of bacteria, fungi or plants which are created when an organism encounters harsh conditions. These spores have a thick cell wall which helps them withstand harsh conditions. They preserve the organism’s genetic material and resume multiplying once favourable conditions are available. The formation of these spores is known as sporulation.

Fission may be defined as the splitting of a unicellular organism into two or more separate daughter cells. There are two kinds of fission binary fission and multiple fission. Binary fission- It is the most common form of reproduction in prokaryotes and occurs in some single-celled eukaryotes.

Apomixis in flowering plants is defined as the asexual formation of a seed from the maternal tissues of the ovule, avoiding the processes of meiosis and fertilization, leading to embryo development. It is derived from the greek words, ‘apo’ and ‘mixis’ which means away from mixing.

options.text: All of these options are correct
options.explanation: Since all of the above-mentioned options, include a single parent and no gamete formation is involved, hence all of the above options are types of asexual reproduction.

questions.explanation: Asexual reproduction is a type of reproduction that involves only one parent, and the offspring produced are genetically identical to the parent. This process does not involve the fusion of gametes (sex cells) and does not contribute to genetic diversity. Asexual reproduction occurs in various forms across different organisms, including bacteria, fungi, plants, and some animals. Methods of asexual reproduction are fission, sporulation, budding, vegetative propagation, artificial propagation, parthenogenesis and apomixis etc.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is incorrect?

options.explanation: Incorrect option.A glass calorimeter is a device used to measure changes in enthalpy (ΔH) during chemical reactions. It works by allowing the reaction to occur within a thermally insulated glass container, and any heat exchanged is reflected in temperature changes. By monitoring these temperature changes, the calorimeter can accurately measure the enthalpy change associated with a reaction, making it a valuable tool for studying energy transformations in chemistry.

options.text: ∆Hc can be determined by bomb calorimeter
options.explanation: Incorrect option.A bomb calorimeter is a specialized device used to determine the heat of combustion (ΔHc) for various substances. It consists of a strong container (the bomb) in which a sample is burned. By measuring the temperature change caused by the combustion, the bomb calorimeter can accurately determine the heat released during the reaction, making it a crucial tool for studying the energy content of fuels and other substances.

questions.text: The types of gametes produced by two pairs of chromosomes can be?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: When considering two pairs of chromosomes, each pair consists of two homologous chromosomes (one from each parent). During meiosis, homologous chromosomes separate, and each haploid cell receives one chromosome from each pair. This results in the production of two types of gametes for each chromosome pair. For example, if we have two pairs of chromosomes, each with two different alleles (Aa and Bb), the possible combinations of gametes would be AB, Ab, aB, and ab.

When two pairs of chromosomes undergo meiosis, the different combinations of maternal and paternal chromosomes lead to four different types of gametes. This is due to the independent assortment of homologous chromosomes during meiosis I. Each homologous pair aligns independently during metaphase I, leading to a random distribution of maternal and paternal chromosomes into the resulting gametes. As a result, four different combinations of alleles are possible for each pair of chromosomes.
Producing six types of gametes from two pairs of chromosomes would be unlikely. Meiosis and independent assortment of chromosomes result in four different combinations of gametes, not six.

Producing eight types of gametes from two pairs of chromosomes is not possible. The maximum number of different gametes that can be produced from two pairs of chromosomes undergoing independent assortment during meiosis is four.

This is due to the independent assortment of homologous chromosomes during meiosis I. As a result, four different combinations of alleles are possible for each pair of chromosomes.

These hormones help prepare the uterus for a potential pregnancy.

questions.text: Gametes in animals are produced by which of the following?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Mitosis is another type of cell division, but it results in the formation of two identical diploid cells. It is responsible for the growth, repair, and maintenance of body tissues but does not produce gametes.

Meiosis is a specialized type of cell division that reduces the chromosome number by half, resulting in the formation of gametes (sperm and egg cells) that are haploid. When these gametes combine during fertilization, the resulting zygote will have a diploid chromosome number.

Fission is a form of asexual reproduction seen in some organisms, but it is not the process by which gametes are produced. In fission, an organism splits into two or more parts, and each part can regenerate into a new individual.

However, it's important to note that the number of eggs a female is born with is finite and decreases over time. Unlike males who continuously produce sperm throughout their reproductive life, females are typically born with a fixed number of immature eggs that gradually decrease through a process known as follicular atresia. During fetal development, oogonia undergo mitotic divisions to increase their numbers. However, by the time a female is born, most of the oogonia have already undergone mitotic divisions and transformed into primary oocytes. Each primary oocyte is surrounded by a layer of cells and is arrested in prophase I of meiosis.

questions.text: Which of the following terms is best defined as a mature, motile, and haploid sperm cell produced during spermatogenesis?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Spermatids are mature but they are not motile.
Spermatogonium is a cell formed at the early stages of the formation of spermatozoa, formed in the walls of the seminiferous tubules and giving rise to spermatocytes by mitosis.
Secondary spermatocytes are haploid cells, smaller than primary spermatocytes and they give rise to spermatids by meiosis.
Spermatozoa are mature, motile male sex cells by which the ovum is fertilized. It has one of more flagella for swimming and has a head containing acrosomes.

questions.explanation: The term that best describes a mature, motile, and haploid sperm cell produced during spermatogenesis is "spermatozoon" or simply "spermatozoa" (plural). A spermatozoon is the final stage of spermatogenesis and represents the functional male gamete capable of fertilizing an egg during sexual reproduction. Spermatozoa are characterized by their streamlined structure, consisting of a head, midpiece, and tail, which enables them to move efficiently toward the egg.

questions.text: Which one of the following surrounds the testicles?

options.explanation: The penis is a male reproductive organ responsible for transferring urine and semen. It does not surround the testicles but is connected to the testicles via the sperm duct.
The urethra is a tube that carries urine from the bladder out of the body. It does not surround the testicles but is connected to the testicles through the sperm duct.
The sperm duct carries sperm from the testicles to the urethra. It is not a surrounding structure for the testicles but a part of the male reproductive system's pathway for sperm transport.

The structure that surrounds the testicles is the scrotum. The scrotum is a pouch of skin and muscle that houses and protects the testes. It is located outside the abdominal cavity, hanging below the penis. The position of the testes outside the body in the scrotum is important for maintaining an optimal temperature for spermatogenesis (sperm production), as the testes require a slightly lower temperature than the rest of the body.

questions.explanation: In flowering plants, one form of parthenogenesis is called apomixis. Apomixis is a mechanism by which plants can reproduce clonally, bypassing the usual process of sexual reproduction that involves the formation of seeds through the fertilization of ovules by pollen. Instead, apomictic plants produce seeds that are genetically identical to the mother plant.

questions.text: A human cell from the ovary has 22 chromosomes and an X chromosome. It is which of the following?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A human cell from the ovary with 22 chromosomes and an X chromosome is an example of a haploid cell. In humans, normal somatic cells are diploid, meaning they have two sets of chromosomes (for a total of 46 chromosomes), with one set inherited from each parent. However, during the process of oogenesis in the ovary, the resulting mature egg (ovum) is haploid.
B is incorrect as Sperm is male gamete and it comes from the testis, the male reproductive organ.
C is incorrect as Somatic cell does not contain the sex chromosomes.

questions.explanation: The mature egg (ovum) contains half the number of chromosomes of a normal somatic cell, which is 23 chromosomes in humans. In this case, if there are 22 autosomes (non-sex chromosomes) and an X chromosome, it is a haploid cell. This haploid egg, when fertilized by a sperm (which also contributes a haploid set of chromosomes), forms a diploid zygote with the normal chromosome complement of 46.

The epididymis is a coiled tube located on the testes where spermatozoa are stored and undergo maturation. It serves as a site for the storage and final maturation of sperm before they are transported through the vas deferens during ejaculation.

questions.text: Which statement is incorrect about ethylene production?

options.text: Climacteric is burst of respiratory activity in fruit ripening
options.explanation: During the climacteric phase, fruits typically exhibit an increase in metabolic activities, including the conversion of starches into sugars, the breakdown of cell walls, and the synthesis of volatile compounds that contribute to the characteristic aroma of the fruit. This phase is crucial for fruits like bananas, apples, tomatoes, and avocados, as it marks the period when they become fully ripe and suitable for consumption.

options.text: It is associated with ethane production
options.explanation: Ethylene is the plant hormone associated with fruit ripening and various other plant growth processes, while ethane is an unrelated hydrocarbon gas found in natural gas reserves.

Ethylene is a key plant hormone that plays a significant role in fruit ripening. It is often referred to as the "ripening hormone" because of its ability to regulate and accelerate the ripening process in various fruits.
Fruit set is assisted by ethylene but it is primarily governed by other plant hormones that regulate the transition from flower to fruit after successful pollination and fertilization.

questions.explanation: Ethylene (C2H4) is a plant hormone, also known as a phytohormone, which plays a crucial role in various physiological processes in plants, including fruit ripening, senescence (aging), and responses to environmental stress. It is a simple hydrocarbon gas and is involved in regulating the growth and development of plants. In the context of fruit ripening, ethylene is responsible for triggering the climacteric phase, as I mentioned in my previous response. On the other hand, ethane (C2H6) is an entirely different hydrocarbon gas, which is an alkane and a component of natural gas. Ethane is not involved in the physiological processes of fruit ripening or any plant-related activities.

questions.text: End of menstrual cycle in old age is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Andropause is a term used to describe a gradual decline in testosterone levels in men as they age. This decline can cause a variety of symptoms, such as decreased sex drive, erectile dysfunction, fatigue, decreased muscle mass, and mood changes. However, unlike menopause, andropause is not a universal experience in men and it is still a subject of debate among medical professionals. Men who are experiencing symptoms of andropause should talk to their healthcare provider to determine the underlying cause and explore treatment options.

Gametopause is a term used to describe the natural decline in reproductive function that occurs in both men and women as they age. In women, this decline is marked by the end of menstrual cycles and the cessation of ovulation, which is known as menopause. In men, the decline is marked by a gradual decrease in testosterone levels and a decline in sperm production. While gametopause is a normal part of aging, it can cause a variety of symptoms and health concerns. Women who experience symptoms of menopause should talk to their healthcare provider about treatment options, while men who are experiencing symptoms of andropause should also consult a healthcare provider.

Menopause is a natural biological process that marks the end of a woman's menstrual cycle. It usually occurs between the ages of 45 and 55, but can occur earlier or later. During menopause, the ovaries stop releasing eggs and the production of hormones such as estrogen and progesterone declines. This can cause a variety of symptoms, such as hot flashes, night sweats, vaginal dryness, mood changes, and sleep disturbances. Menopause also increases the risk of certain health conditions such as osteoporosis and heart disease. While menopause is a normal part of aging, it is important to talk to a healthcare provider about any symptoms or concerns.
This option is incorrect as the correct answer is C.

questions.text: The hormone that is released from the testes is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The testes do not produce progesterone. Progesterone is a hormone that is primarily produced by the corpus luteum in the ovaries in females, although it is also produced in smaller amounts by the adrenal glands and by the testes in males. However, the amount of progesterone produced by the testes is relatively small compared to the amount produced by the ovaries in females. Testosterone is the primary male sex hormone that is produced by the Leydig cells of the testes.

The testes do not produce estrogen. Estrogen is a hormone that is primarily produced by the ovaries in females, although it is also produced in small amounts by the adrenal glands and by adipose (fat) tissue. Testosterone, on the other hand, is the primary male sex hormone that is produced by the Leydig cells of the testes. While testosterone can be converted into estrogen by the enzyme aromatase in certain tissues, such as adipose tissue, the testes themselves do not produce estrogen.

The hormone released from testes is testosterone. Testosterone is a steroid hormone that is produced by the Leydig cells of the testes. It plays an important role in the development of male reproductive tissues and secondary sexual characteristics, such as increased muscle mass, body hair, and a deepening voice. Testosterone also regulates a number of physiological processes in males, including bone density, fat distribution, and red blood cell production.
Testes cannot produce all the above mentioned hormones hence this option is not correct.

questions.explanation: The hormone released from the testes is testosterone. Testosterone is a steroid hormone that is produced by the Leydig cells of the testes. It plays an important role in the development of male reproductive tissues and secondary sexual characteristics, such as increased muscle mass, body hair, and a deepening voice. Testosterone also regulates several physiological processes in males, including bone density, fat distribution, and red blood cell production.

questions.text: When a female ovulates, in which phase of the division is the oocyte?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: During anaphase I, the homologous chromosomes are separated and move to opposite poles of the cell. This does not occur during ovulation, so this is not the correct answer.

During prophase I, the chromosomes condense and the nuclear envelope breaks down. This does occur during ovulation, but it is only the first stage of meiosis. The oocyte is in metaphase II of meiosis during ovulation, so this is not the correct answer.

During metaphase I, the chromosomes line up along the middle of the cell. This does occur during ovulation, but it is only the second stage of meiosis. The oocyte is in metaphase II of meiosis during ovulation, so this is not the correct answer.
After ovulation, the oocyte is arrested in metaphase of meiosis II until fertilization.

questions.explanation: After ovulation, the oocyte is arrested in metaphase of meiosis II until fertilization.

questions.text: In 'cloning', nucleus is introduced into which of the following?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The nucleus is inserted into an egg cell during cloning. Cloning is removing the nucleus from an egg cell and replacing it with a somatic cell from the animal that is being cloned, such as a skin or blood cell. The resulting repaired egg cell is stimulated to divide and form an embryo because it now has the entire set of genetic instructions from the donor animal.

The nucleus is inserted into an egg cell during cloning. Cloning is removing the nucleus from an egg cell and replacing it with a somatic cell from the animal that is being cloned, such as a skin or blood cell. The resulting repaired egg cell is stimulated to divide and form an embryo because it now has the entire set of genetic instructions from the donor animal.
Sperm cells only contain half of the genetic information needed to form a complete organism, so they cannot be used on their own to create a cloned animal.

Zygotes, which are formed by the fusion of a sperm cell and an egg cell during fertilization, contain the complete set of genetic instructions for an organism. However, they are not typically used in the process of cloning because they have already undergone some degree of development and differentiation.

questions.explanation: Option B is correct since in animals and especially among vertebrates, a nucleus from somatic cells is removed and introduced into an egg cell, whose own nucleus is destroyed by ultraviolet radiations.Moreover, through this method, the egg develops into an organism, genetically identical to the parent who has contributed the nucleus.

questions.text: Where in the female reproductive system are thousands of eggs found?

options.explanation: Thousands of eggs are found in the ovaries of the female reproductive system.

The vagina is a part of the female reproductive system, but it does not contain thousands of eggs. The vagina is a muscular canal that connects the external genitalia to the cervix of the uterus and serves as the entry point for the penis during sexual intercourse and the exit point for menstrual flow.

The fallopian tubes, also known as oviducts, are a pair of slender tubes that extend from the ovaries to the uterus. They provide a pathway for the eggs to travel from the ovaries to the uterus after ovulation. While the fallopian tubes play a crucial role in fertilization, they do not store thousands of eggs.
The uterus, also known as the womb, is the organ where a fertilized egg implants and develops into a fetus during pregnancy. It is not the site where thousands of eggs are found.

questions.explanation: Option A is correct since thousands of eggs are found in the ovaries of the female reproductive system.The ovaries are a pair of small, almond-shaped organs located on either side of the uterus in the female reproductive system.

In honey bees, males, also known as drones, are haploid. Haploid means they possess a single set of chromosomes, as they develop from unfertilized eggs. questions.text: Second meiotic division in the secondary oocyte proceeds as far as :

Prophase is the initial stage of cell division during which chromosomes condense and become visible. In the context of meiosis, prophase II involves the reformation of spindle fibers and the separation of sister chromatids.

Anaphase is a stage of cell division in which sister chromatids are separated and pulled toward opposite poles of the cell. In the context of meiosis, anaphase II involves the separation of sister chromatids.

questions.text: Which one of the following differentiates directly into mature sperm?

Spermatids are haploid cells that result from the second meiotic division of secondary spermatocytes. They undergo a process called spermiogenesis, which involves extensive structural and morphological changes to transform into mature sperm.

questions.text: Breakdown of endometrium during menstruation is due to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Luteinizing hormone (LH) is a hormone that is produced by the pituitary gland. It is responsible for stimulating ovulation, the release of an egg cell from the ovary. An increase in LH levels can cause a number of changes in the body, e.g LH triggers ovulation, the release of an egg cell from the ovary. LH stimulates the production of progesterone by the corpus luteum, the remnant of the ovary that released the egg cell. LH stimulates the growth of the endometrium, the lining of the uterus.

The breakdown of the endometrium during menstruation is due to a decrease in the levels of the hormone progesterone. This hormone is responsible for maintaining the lining of the uterus, and when its levels decrease, the lining begins to break down.

Progesterone is a hormone that is produced by the ovaries. It is responsible for preparing the uterus for pregnancy. An increase in progesterone levels can cause a number of changes in the body, including, changes in the menstrual cycle. Progesterone helps to regulate the menstrual cycle. An increase in progesterone levels can lead to longer menstrual cycles, or to heavier periods. Changes in the breasts e.g progesterone can cause the breasts to become fuller and more tender. It can also lead to the development of breast milk in pregnant women. Changes in the skin e.g Progesterone can cause the skin to become drier and more sensitive. It can also lead to the development of acne. Changes in the mood e.g progesterone can affect the mood, causing some women to feel more emotional or irritable.

A rise in estrogen levels can cause a number of changes in the body, including: Changes in the menstrual cycle: Estrogen helps to regulate the menstrual cycle. An increase in estrogen levels can lead to shorter or longer menstrual cycles, or to heavier or lighter periods. Changes in the breasts, estrogen can cause the breasts to grow and become more sensitive. Changes in the skin, estrogen can cause the skin to become thicker and more oily. It can also lead to the development of acne. Changes in the hair, estrogen can cause the hair to become thicker and darker. It can also lead to the growth of hair in places where it is not normally found, such as the face or chin. Changes in the mood, estrogen can affect the mood, causing some women to feel more emotional or irritable.

questions.explanation: The breakdown of the endometrium during menstruation is due to a decrease in the levels of the hormone progesterone. This hormone is responsible for maintaining the lining of the uterus, and when its levels decrease, the lining begins to break down. So option B is correct.

questions.text: Which of the following is a typical symptom of AIDS?

options.explanation: Sudden weight loss is a typical symptom of AIDS. It shows complex symptoms such as severe pneumonia , a rare vascular cancer, sudden weight loss, swollen lymph nodes and general loss of immune functions.

questions.explanation: Stage 1: Acute HIV Infection Within 2 to 4 weeks after infection with HIV, about two-thirds of people will have a flu-like illness. This is the body’s natural response to HIV infection. Flu-like symptoms can include:Fever, Chills,Rash,Night sweats,Muscle aches,Sore throat,Fatigue,Swollen lymph nodes,Mouth ulcersThese symptoms can last anywhere from a few days to several weeks. But some people do not have any symptoms at all during this early stage of HIV.Stage 2: Clinical Latency In this stage, the virus still multiplies, but at very low levels. People in this stage may not feel sick or have any symptoms. This stage is also called chronic HIV infection.Without HIV treatment, people can stay in this stage for 10 or 15 years, but some move through this stage faster.If you take HIV medicine exactly as prescribed and get and keep an undetectable viral load, you can live and long and healthy life and will not transmit HIV to your HIV-negative partners through sexStage 3: AIDSIf you have HIV and you are not on HIV treatment, eventually the virus will weaken your body’s immune system and you will progress to AIDS (acquired immunodeficiency syndrome).This is the late stage of HIV infection.Symptoms of AIDS can include Rapid weight loss, Recurring fever or profuse night sweats, Extreme and unexplained tiredness, Prolonged swelling of the lymph glands in the armpits, groin, or neck, Diarrhea that lasts for more than a week, Sores of the mouth, anus, or genitals, Pneumonia, Red, brown, pink, or purplish blotches on or under the skin or inside the mouth, nose, or eyelids, Memory loss, depression, and other neurologic disorders.

options.explanation: Invitro fertilization is the technique in which an egg is fertilized by sperm in the tube outside the body. Sperm with poor motility can be used to fertilize eggs through IVF. questions.text: All of the following are sexually transmitted diseases except:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Syphilis is a sexually transmitted infection (STI) caused by the bacterium Treponema pallidum. It is typically spread through sexual contact, including vaginal, anal, or oral sex. Syphilis progresses through different stages and can cause a wide range of symptoms, including sores or ulcers, rash, fever, and flu-like symptoms. If left untreated, it can lead to serious complications affecting the heart, brain, and other organs.

Gonorrhea is another common STI caused by the bacterium Neisseria gonorrhoeae. It is transmitted through sexual contact, including vaginal, anal, or oral sex. Symptoms of gonorrhea may include a burning sensation during urination, abnormal discharge from the genitals, and in some cases, no symptoms at all. If left untreated, gonorrhea can lead to complications such as pelvic inflammatory disease (in women) and infertility.

Alzheimer disease is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern , which means one copy of an altered gene in each cell is sufficient to cause the disorder. In most cases, an affected person inherits the altered gene from one affected parent.

Genital herpes is a viral infection caused by the herpes simplex virus (HSV), usually HSV type 2. It is transmitted through sexual contact, including vaginal, anal, or oral sex. Genital herpes can cause painful sores or blisters in the genital area, as well as flu-like symptoms such as fever and swollen lymph nodes. The virus can remain dormant in the body and cause recurrent outbreaks throughout a person's life, although symptoms can be managed with antiviral medications.

AIDS (Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome): AIDS is not a sexually transmitted infection itself but rather a condition caused by the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV). HIV is primarily transmitted through unprotected sexual contact, sharing contaminated needles, or from mother to child during childbirth or breastfeeding. If left untreated, HIV can lead to a weakened immune system, making individuals susceptible to various infections and certain types of cancer. AIDS is the final stage of HIV infection, characterized by severe immune system damage and the presence of opportunistic infections or cancers.

questions.explanation: Alzheimer’s Disease is not a sexually transmitted disease. It is a genetic disease. Having a genetic variant of the apolipoprotein E (APOE) gene on chromosome 19 increases a person's risk of having Alzheimer’s Disease.

questions.text: Menstrual cycle can be divided into
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The menstrual cycle can be divided into two cycles: the ovarian cycle and the uterine cycle. The ovarian cycle involves the development and release of the egg, while the uterine cycle involves the thickening of the endometrium in preparation for pregnancy and the shedding of the endometrium if pregnancy does not occur. These two cycles are coordinated by hormones, including estrogen and progesterone, and are typically around 28 days in length, although the length can vary between individuals.

questions.explanation: The menstrual cycle is governed by hormonal changes. These changes can be altered by using hormonal birth control to prevent pregnancy. Each cycle can be divided into two phases based on events in the ovary (ovarian cycle) or in the uterus (uterine cycle).

questions.text: Colour of corpus luteum is:

options.explanation: The corpus luteum is not red. If it appears red, it may be due to bleeding or inflammation.

The color of the corpus luteum is yellow. The name "corpus luteum" literally means "yellow body" in Latin. The yellow color is due to the presence of carotenoids, which are pigments that are also found in fruits and vegetables. Carotenoids are antioxidants that help protect the body from damage. The corpus luteum produces progesterone, a hormone that is important for maintaining pregnancy. The yellow color of the corpus luteum is a sign that it is producing progesterone.The corpus luteum is a temporary structure that forms after ovulation. It typically lasts for about 12-14 days, but it can last for up to 21 days. If pregnancy does not occur, the corpus luteum will eventually degenerate and become a scar called the corpus albicans. The corpus albicans is white in color.
The corpus luteum is not grey. If it appears grey, it may be due to a lack of blood flow or a degenerative process.
The corpus luteum is not black. If it appears black, it may be due to a tumor or a blood clot.

questions.text: In asexual reproduction, offsprings are genetically?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: It is wrong because if mutations occur dissimilarities will arise.

options.text: Identical if mutations do not occur
options.explanation: In asexual reproduction, offspring are genetically identical or clones of the parent organism. This means that the genetic makeup of the offspring is an exact copy of the genetic makeup of the parent.Asexual reproduction involves the production of offspring without the involvement of gametes (sperm and egg cells) or fertilization. Instead, a single parent organism gives rise to one or more genetically identical offspring through various mechanisms such as budding, fission, fragmentation, or parthenogenesis.Since there is no genetic recombination or mixing of genetic material from two different parents in asexual reproduction, the offspring inherit all their genetic material from a single parent, resulting in genetic uniformity. While asexual reproduction has the advantage of being a rapid and efficient way to produce offspring, it may also limit the adaptability of a population to changing environments since there is no genetic diversity introduced through sexual reproduction.
It is wrong because in most cases asexual reproduction will give genetically identical offspring.
It is wrong.

questions.explanation: In asexual reproduction, offspring are genetically identical or clones of the parent organism. This means that the genetic makeup of the offspring is an exact copy of the genetic makeup of the parent.

The uterus is incorrect because sponges do not have a uterus. The uterus is a specialized organ found in higher animals that serves to protect and nourish developing embryos.

Some sponge species reproduce sexually. These are mostly hermaphrodite, mostly protandrous, i.e. male sex cells develop first. In some sponges the sexes are separate. Sperms released in water are carried to the eggs by amoeboid cells. Fertilization occurs in mesenchyme and zygote is formed. The embryo development includes blastula and larval stages.

questions.text: The shared terminal duct of the reproductive and urinary system in the human male is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The shared terminal duct of the reproductive and urinary systems in the human male is the urethra. The urethra is a tube that serves as a common passageway for both urine and semen to exit the body. It extends from the urinary bladder, where it carries urine from the bladder to the outside of the body during urination, to the tip of the penis, where it allows the ejaculation of semen during sexual intercourse.
Ureters are not present in males.
Vas deferens does not carry the urine. It is only part of the reproductive system.
Vasa efferentia does not carry the urine. It is only part of the reproductive system.

questions.explanation: Urethra is the urinary duct which originates from the neck of urinary bladder and opens to the exterior at the tip of penis in males. It is a common pathway for passage of urine and semen.

questions.text: The main function of mammalian corpus luteum is to produce:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The corpus luteum does produce some estrogen, but its primary output is progesterone.

The main function of the mammalian corpus luteum is to produce progesterone. The corpus luteum forms from the ruptured follicle after ovulation and plays a crucial role in preparing the uterine lining for implantation of a fertilized egg. While it produces some estrogen, its primary product is progesterone, which helps maintain the uterine lining and support early pregnancy.
The corpus luteum is not responsible for producing hCG. hCG is produced by the developing placenta during pregnancy.
While the corpus luteum does produce some relaxin, its main hormone production is progesterone, crucial for the early stages of pregnancy.

questions.explanation: Corpus luteum secretes steroid hormones progesterone and estrogen, to make uterus suitable Corpus luteum secretes steroid hormones progesterone and estrogen, to make uterus suitable for implantation (in case fertilisation occurs) and it’s maintenance (mainly endometrium).

questions.text: Menstrual flow occurs due to lack of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Oxytocin is a hormone produced by the hypothalamus and released by the posterior pituitary gland. In the context of the menstrual cycle, oxytocin is not directly involved in regulating the cycle itself. Instead, it plays a role during childbirth and lactation. During labor, oxytocin is responsible for stimulating uterine contractions, helping with the progression of labor and delivery.

Vasopressin, also known as antidiuretic hormone (ADH), is not directly involved in the menstrual flow either. Its primary functions in the body are related to regulating water balance and blood pressure.

Progesterone is involved in the regulation of the menstrual flow. After ovulation, the ruptured ovarian follicle transforms into the corpus luteum, which produces progesterone. Progesterone's main role is to maintain the thickened uterine lining (endometrium) that was prepared during the first half of the menstrual cycle. If fertilization does not occur, the corpus luteum regresses, leading to a drop in progesterone levels. This drop in progesterone triggers the shedding of the uterine lining, which results in the menstrual flow, also known as menstruation or a period. The menstrual flow is the discharge of blood and tissue from the uterus through the vagina, marking the beginning of a new menstrual cycle.

FSH plays a role in the menstrual cycle by stimulating the growth and development of ovarian follicles in the ovaries. Each ovarian follicle contains an immature egg (oocyte). During the first half of the menstrual cycle, known as the follicular phase, FSH levels rise, and multiple follicles start to grow. As the follicles develop, they produce increasing amounts of estrogen.

questions.explanation: The corpus luteum secretes large amounts of progesterone which is essential for maintenance of the endometrium. Such as endometrium is necessary for implantation of the fertilized ovum and other events of pregnancy. In the absence of fertilization, the corpus luteum degenerates. This causes disintegration of the endometrium leading to menstruation. The menstrual flow results due to breakdown of endometrial lining of the uterus and its blood vessels which forms liquid that comes out through vagina.

questions.text: The secretory phase in the human menstrual cycle is also called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: After ovulation, the ruptured follicle transforms into a temporary endocrine structure called the corpus luteum. The corpus luteum secretes hormones, primarily progesterone and some estrogen, during the luteal phase. These hormones help prepare the uterus for potential implantation of a fertilized egg and support early pregnancy.

This phase begins on the first day of menstruation (bleeding) and lasts until ovulation. During this phase, follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) from the pituitary gland stimulates the growth and development of ovarian follicles. Each follicle contains an immature egg (ovum), but usually, only one dominant follicle continues to develop while others degenerate.

Around the middle of the menstrual cycle (approximately day 14 in a 28-day cycle), a surge of luteinizing hormone (LH) triggers the release of the mature egg from the dominant follicle. This is known as ovulation, and the egg is released into the fallopian tube, where it can potentially be fertilized by sperm.

Menses, also known as menstruation or the menstrual period, is the shedding of the uterine lining (endometrium) that occurs in females of reproductive age who are not pregnant. It is a normal and natural part of the menstrual cycle, which is the monthly series of hormonal changes and events that occur in the female reproductive system.

questions.explanation: After ovulation which occurs in the middle of the menstrual cycle, the empty Graafian follicle continues growth under the influence of LH. The follicular cells are converted into lutein cells by deposition of yellowish lipid inclusions. The phenomenon is called luteinization. The ruptured Graafian follicle is now called the corpus luteum. It secretes hormones, mainly progesterone and a small quantity of estrogen. Both LH and progesterone help in further growth and thickening of endometrium. The major change is that the endometrial glands become secretory. The uterine wall becomes ready for nourishing and anchoring blastocyst if fertilization takes place. Hence, this phase of the menstrual cycle is called the luteal or secretory phase. The phase lasts for about 13 days.

questions.text: If for some reason, the vasa efferentia in the human reproductive system get blocked, the gametes will not be transported from:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The male sex accessory ducts include rete testis, vasa efferentia, epididymis and vas deferens. The seminiferous tubules of the testis open into the vasa efferentia through rete testis. The vasa efferentia leave the testis and open into epididymis located along the posterior surface of each testis. So if vasa efferentia gets blocked, the gametes will not be transported from testes to epididymis.

Adrenal cortex cells, specifically the cells of the zona fasciculata, are responsible for producing cortisol, aldosterone, and other steroid hormones—not adrenaline (also known as epinephrine), which is primarily secreted by the adrenal medulla.

options.text: Seminiferous tubules and provide nutrition to germ cells
options.explanation: This option is correct. Sertoli cells are specialized cells found in the seminiferous tubules of the testes. They play a crucial role in supporting and nourishing the developing germ cells (sperm cells) during spermatogenesis, which is the process of sperm production. Sertoli cells provide physical support, produce essential proteins and growth factors, regulate hormone levels, and create a suitable microenvironment for the maturation of sperm cells.

This option is incorrect. Sertoli cells are not found in the pancreas. The pancreas contains other cell types, such as exocrine cells that produce digestive enzymes and endocrine cells, specifically the pancreatic islet cells, which secrete hormones such as insulin and glucagon.
